name: Richard M. Scrushy
charges_filed: 36 counts of conspiracy, securities fraud, wire fraud, money laundering, and false certifications under Sarbanes-Oxley Act (first-ever SOX criminal prosecution); original indictment included 85 counts
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Northern District of Alabama (Birmingham) — Federal
resolution_details: Jury acquitted Scrushy on all 36 remaining counts after 21 days of deliberations spanning roughly one month. Jurors cited lack of a 'smoking gun' directly linking Scrushy to the $2.7 billion accounting fraud and credibility problems with the five former HealthSouth CFOs who testified against him under plea agreements. He was the first CEO charged under Sarbanes-Oxley. Judge Karon O. Bowdre had previously dismissed three perjury counts and an obstruction count during trial. NOTE: Scrushy was later separately charged and convicted of bribery/corruption in an unrelated 2006 Montgomery, Alabama trial (United States v. Siegelman); that conviction is NOT the subject of this entry.
source1_description: DOJ Press Release — HealthSouth Founder and Former CEO Richard Scrushy Charged in $2.7 Billion Accounting Fraud Conspiracy (November 4, 2003)
source2_description: New York Times — HealthSouth Founder Acquitted of All Counts of Corporate Fraud (June 28, 2005)
notes: Scrushy was the first CEO charged under Sarbanes-Oxley. His strategy of becoming a televangelist in Birmingham — where 11 of 18 potential jurors were African American — was widely discussed. Fifteen former HealthSouth executives pleaded guilty; all testified against him. Despite acquittal in the Birmingham fraud case, he was subsequently convicted in the separate Montgomery bribery case in 2006. That separate conviction is documented in the convictions database.
disclaimer: CHARGED ONLY — NOT CONVICTED. Charges were filed but did not result in a conviction. (Note: acquitted of the HealthSouth fraud charges specifically; separately convicted in unrelated bribery matter.)

name: Ralph R. Cioffi
role_title: Senior Portfolio Manager (Senior Managing Director, Bear Stearns Asset Management)
company: Bear Stearns Asset Management (Bear Stearns & Co.)
charges_filed: Conspiracy to commit securities fraud and wire fraud; securities fraud (3 counts); wire fraud (2 counts); insider trading (1 count) — 6 total counts
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Eastern District of New York (Brooklyn) — Federal
resolution_details: Jury acquitted Cioffi and co-defendant Tannin on all charges after approximately eight hours of deliberation over two days. Jurors said the evidence was flimsy and contradictory, and that the defendants may have made poor investment decisions but that did not constitute criminal fraud. The government alleged that Cioffi and Tannin deliberately misled investors about the deteriorating condition of two Bear Stearns hedge funds heavily exposed to subprime mortgages before those funds collapsed in June 2007, causing approximately $1.6 billion in investor losses. This was the first major criminal trial arising from the 2008 subprime mortgage crisis and its outcome set back the government's prosecution strategy.
source1_description: DOJ / U.S. Attorney's Office (EDNY) Press Release — Two Senior Managers of Failed Bear Stearns Hedge Funds Indicted on Conspiracy and Fraud Charges (June 19, 2008)
source2_description: New York Times — 2 Bear Stearns Fund Leaders Are Acquitted (November 10, 2009)
notes: This was widely considered the flagship prosecution arising from the 2008 financial crisis. The acquittal was seen as a major setback for DOJ's efforts to hold individual financial executives criminally accountable for the crisis. Cioffi was also separately charged with insider trading for transferring $2 million from one failing fund to another while misrepresenting his position to investors — he was acquitted of this as well. Both defendants also faced related SEC civil charges.
disclaimer: CHARGED ONLY — NOT CONVICTED. Charges were filed but did not result in a conviction.

name: Matthew M. Tannin
role_title: Portfolio Manager and Chief Operating Officer, Bear Stearns Asset Management
company: Bear Stearns Asset Management (Bear Stearns & Co.)
charges_filed: Conspiracy to commit securities fraud and wire fraud; securities fraud (3 counts); wire fraud (2 counts) — 5 total counts
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Eastern District of New York (Brooklyn) — Federal
resolution_details: Jointly tried with Ralph Cioffi and acquitted on all counts on the second day of jury deliberations. Jurors stated that there was 'reasonable doubt regarding every charge' and that they simply did not believe the case was proven. Prosecutors alleged Tannin misled investors about the health of two Bear Stearns subprime-heavy hedge funds in the months before their collapse in 2007. A notable element: a key email from Tannin to Cioffi stating the subprime market looked 'pretty damn ugly' was at first successfully suppressed (due to a deficient search warrant), though ultimately admitted; its introduction did not lead to conviction.
source1_description: DOJ / U.S. Attorney's Office (EDNY) Press Release — Two Senior Managers of Failed Bear Stearns Hedge Funds Indicted on Conspiracy and Fraud Charges (June 19, 2008)
source2_description: Reuters — Jury Acquits Ex-Bear Stearns Hedge Fund Managers (November 10, 2009)
notes: Tried jointly with Cioffi. The acquittal of both men was broadly described by legal analysts as a signal that the government would face serious difficulty prosecuting complex financial fraud cases where intent to defraud — rather than poor judgment — had to be proven beyond a reasonable doubt.
disclaimer: CHARGED ONLY — NOT CONVICTED. Charges were filed but did not result in a conviction.

name: Frank P. Quattrone
charges_filed: Three counts: (1) Obstruction of justice (18 U.S.C. §§ 1503 & 2) — corruptly endeavoring to obstruct a grand jury proceeding; (2) Obstruction of an SEC investigation (18 U.S.C. §§ 1505 & 2); (3) Witness tampering (18 U.S.C. §§ 1512 & 2) — alleged document destruction directive
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Southern District of New York — Federal
case_number: Not publicly reported as a standalone docket number (SDNY, filed April 2003)
resolution_details: Quattrone's first trial in 2003 ended in a hung jury. He was convicted at a second trial in 2004 and sentenced to 18 months in prison, but the Second Circuit Court of Appeals reversed the conviction in March 2006, ruling the jury had received improper instructions about the required nexus between Quattrone's conduct and pending investigations. The government chose not to pursue a third trial and instead entered a deferred prosecution agreement with Quattrone in August 2006. All charges were formally dismissed with prejudice by U.S. District Judge George Daniels on August 30, 2007, after Quattrone completed the 12-month period under the agreement without incident. Quattrone never served any prison time.
source1_description: Reuters — U.S. Judge Drops Obstruction Charges Against Quattrone (August 30, 2007)
source2_description: New York Times — After 4 Years, Last Charges Dropped in Quattrone Case (August 30, 2007)
notes: Quattrone was one of the most prominent technology investment bankers of the dot-com era, responsible for taking Amazon, Cisco, and other major companies public. He was accused of sending an email in December 2000 to CSFB staff saying 'time to clean up those files,' which the government alleged was a directive to destroy documents subject to grand jury and SEC subpoenas. Quattrone contended the email was merely a reminder to follow standard company document retention policy.
disclaimer: CHARGED ONLY — NOT CONVICTED. Charges were filed but did not result in a conviction.

name: David I. Rainey
role_title: Vice President of Exploration, Gulf of Mexico; Deputy Incident Commander during spill response
company: BP Exploration and Production, Inc. (BP plc)
charges_filed: Count 1: Obstruction of Congress (18 U.S.C. § 1505) — alleged obstruction of congressional subcommittee investigation into oil spill flow rates; Count 2: Making false statements to federal law enforcement (FBI) (18 U.S.C. § 1001)
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Eastern District of Louisiana (New Orleans) — Federal
resolution_details: Rainey was the highest-ranking BP executive charged in connection with the April 2010 Deepwater Horizon disaster, which killed 11 workers and caused the largest offshore oil spill in U.S. history. He was accused of deliberately understating oil flow rates to Congress (reporting ~5,000 barrels/day when internal estimates ran as high as 146,000 barrels/day). At trial in June 2015, U.S. District Judge Kurt Englehardt dismissed the obstruction of Congress charge on the first day of trial, ruling that congressional witnesses' invocation of the Speech or Debate Clause prevented the defense from mounting a fair case and that no adequate authorization for the congressional inquiry was established. Five days later, after roughly two hours of deliberation, the jury acquitted Rainey of the remaining false-statements count.
source1_description: DOJ Press Release — BP Exploration and Production Inc. Agrees to Plead Guilty — includes individual charges against Rainey (November 15, 2012)
source2_description: PBS NewsHour — Former BP exec acquitted on charges of lying to investigators after Deepwater Horizon oil spill (June 6, 2015)
notes: Rainey's acquittal was the final in a series of setbacks for DOJ's individual prosecution effort in the Deepwater Horizon disaster. The obstruction charge was dismissed due to the Speech or Debate Clause preventing congressional witnesses from being compelled to testify in Rainey's defense — a constitutional collision the court resolved in Rainey's favor. BP the corporation pleaded guilty to 14 criminal counts and paid $4 billion in criminal fines.
disclaimer: CHARGED ONLY — NOT CONVICTED. Charges were filed but did not result in a conviction.

name: Robert M. Kaluza
role_title: Well Site Leader (highest-ranking BP supervisor aboard the Deepwater Horizon at time of explosion)
company: BP Exploration and Production, Inc. (BP plc)
charges_filed: 23 counts total: 11 counts of seaman's manslaughter (18 U.S.C. § 1115), 11 counts of involuntary manslaughter (18 U.S.C. § 1112), and 1 count of violating the Clean Water Act — for alleged negligence that caused the explosion killing 11 workers
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Eastern District of Louisiana (New Orleans) — Federal
resolution_details: All serious felony charges were eliminated before or during trial through a sequence of dismissals: (1) In 2013, the district court (Judge Duval) dismissed all 11 seaman's manslaughter counts, ruling Kaluza and Vidrine were not 'ship officers' within the scope of the 1838 statute; (2) The Fifth Circuit affirmed this dismissal in March 2015; (3) In December 2015, DOJ voluntarily dropped all 11 involuntary manslaughter counts, citing insufficient evidence to prove 'wanton or reckless disregard for life'; (4) On February 25, 2016, a federal jury acquitted Kaluza of the sole remaining misdemeanor Clean Water Act violation in under two hours. Kaluza's co-defendant Donald Vidrine (see separate entry) pleaded guilty to the Clean Water Act misdemeanor.
source1_description: DOJ Criminal Division case page — United States v. BP Exploration and Production, Inc. (includes individual defendants Kaluza and Vidrine)
source2_description: Seattle Times / AP — Jury finds former BP engineer not guilty in oil spill case (February 25, 2016)
notes: BP the corporation pleaded guilty to crimes based in part on the negligence of Kaluza and Vidrine, placing BP in the unusual position of having admitted criminal liability tied to charges against its employees that courts and juries then rejected. No individual BP employee ultimately served prison time for the deaths of 11 workers.
disclaimer: CHARGED ONLY — NOT CONVICTED. Charges were filed but did not result in a conviction.

name: Donald J. Vidrine
role_title: Well Site Leader (highest-ranking BP supervisor aboard the Deepwater Horizon at time of explosion)
company: BP Exploration and Production, Inc. (BP plc)
charges_filed: 23 counts total (same as Kaluza): 11 counts of seaman's manslaughter (18 U.S.C. § 1115), 11 counts of involuntary manslaughter (18 U.S.C. § 1112), and 1 count of violating the Clean Water Act
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Eastern District of Louisiana (New Orleans) — Federal
resolution_details: The 11 seaman's manslaughter counts were dismissed by the district court in 2013 (affirmed by the Fifth Circuit in March 2015). All 11 involuntary manslaughter counts were voluntarily dropped by DOJ on December 2, 2015 'in the interests of justice,' after civil case testimony convinced prosecutors they could not prove gross negligence. Vidrine pleaded guilty on the same day to the single remaining misdemeanor Clean Water Act count and was sentenced to 10 months of probation, 100 hours of community service, and $50,000 in restitution — no prison time.
source1_description: NBC News — Manslaughter Charges Dropped Against Deepwater Horizon Supervisor (December 2, 2015)
source2_description: Reuters — BP Spill Manslaughter Charges Dropped, One Guilty of Environmental Crime (December 2, 2015)
notes: Like Kaluza, Vidrine was accused of misreading a critical negative pressure test on the Deepwater Horizon well and ignoring warning signs of an imminent blowout on April 20, 2010. The manslaughter charges required proof of gross negligence ('wanton disregard for life'); testimony in civil proceedings made prosecutors conclude this standard could not be met. Vidrine's guilty plea to a single misdemeanor is NOT a felony conviction — this entry covers the dismissed manslaughter charges only.
disclaimer: CHARGED ONLY — NOT CONVICTED on the criminal manslaughter charges. All manslaughter charges (felony) were dismissed. Pleaded guilty to a separate misdemeanor Clean Water Act violation (not a manslaughter or felony conviction).

name: Anthony Allen
role_title: Global Head of Liquidity and Finance (USD LIBOR Submitter)
charges_filed: Wire fraud conspiracy (18 U.S.C. § 1349); wire fraud (18 U.S.C. § 1343); bank fraud conspiracy — in connection with alleged manipulation of USD LIBOR rate submissions to benefit Rabobank's derivatives trading positions, approximately 2006–2011
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Southern District of New York — Federal (indictment September 2014; original trial November 2015)
case_number: United States v. Allen, No. 16-898 (2d Cir. 2017) / S.D.N.Y.
resolution_details: Allen and Conti were UK citizens tried in absentia in New York, convicted in November 2015 after trial in the Southern District of New York (Judge Jed Rakoff). The Second Circuit reversed both convictions and dismissed the indictments entirely on July 19, 2017, holding that the Fifth Amendment's privilege against self-incrimination prohibited the government from using testimony compelled by a foreign sovereign (the UK's Financial Conduct Authority) against the defendants in U.S. proceedings. The government's key cooperating witness, Paul Robson, had been exposed to Allen and Conti's compelled FCA testimony before testifying for the prosecution, and the Second Circuit found the government failed to show this exposure did not taint the evidence.
source1_description: United States v. Allen, No. 16-898 (2d Cir. July 19, 2017) — Second Circuit opinion on Justia
source2_description: New York Times — Convictions of 2 Former Traders in Libor Scandal Are Dismissed (July 19, 2017)
notes: This was the first U.S. criminal conviction in the LIBOR manipulation investigation, and its reversal was a significant turning point. The Second Circuit's ruling established that the Fifth Amendment applies to foreign-compelled testimony when used in U.S. criminal proceedings, setting an important constitutional precedent for cross-border financial prosecutions.
disclaimer: CHARGED ONLY — NOT CONVICTED. Charges were filed but did not result in a conviction. (Initial conviction was reversed and indictment dismissed by Second Circuit.)

name: Anthony Conti
charges_filed: Wire fraud conspiracy (18 U.S.C. § 1349); wire fraud (18 U.S.C. § 1343); bank fraud conspiracy — in connection with alleged manipulation of USD LIBOR submissions at Rabobank
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Southern District of New York — Federal
case_number: United States v. Allen, No. 16-898 (2d Cir. 2017) / S.D.N.Y.
resolution_details: Tried jointly with Anthony Allen; original conviction in November 2015 reversed and indictment dismissed by the Second Circuit on July 19, 2017 on identical Fifth Amendment grounds (compelled FCA testimony used against defendants via government cooperating witness Paul Robson). See Anthony Allen entry for full analysis.
source1_description: United States v. Allen, No. 16-898 (2d Cir. July 19, 2017) — Second Circuit opinion on Justia
source2_description: Reuters — U.S. Appeals Court Voids Libor Convictions of Ex-Rabobank Traders (July 19, 2017)
notes: UK citizen tried in the U.S. The Rabobank LIBOR case was the first brought to trial in the U.S. DOJ LIBOR investigation; the reversal of its convictions was part of a broader pattern that ultimately saw every single U.S. trial-level conviction in LIBOR-related cases overturned.
disclaimer: CHARGED ONLY — NOT CONVICTED. Charges were filed but did not result in a conviction. (Initial conviction was reversed and indictment dismissed by Second Circuit.)

name: Matthew Connolly
charges_filed: Conspiracy to commit wire fraud and bank fraud (18 U.S.C. § 1349); wire fraud (18 U.S.C. § 1343) — alleged manipulation of Deutsche Bank's USD LIBOR submissions to benefit derivatives trading positions, 2005–2011
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Southern District of New York — Federal; appeal: U.S. Court of Appeals, Second Circuit
case_number: United States v. Connolly, No. 19-3806 (2d Cir. 2022) / No. 19-3945
resolution_details: Connolly and Gavin Black were convicted at trial in October 2018 (Judge Colleen McMahon, SDNY) after the jury found they induced Deutsche Bank's LIBOR submitters to alter rate submissions to benefit the bank's derivatives book. The Second Circuit reversed both convictions on January 27, 2022, ordering judgments of acquittal entered — holding that the government failed to prove the LIBOR submissions were false or deceptive. The court reasoned that because LIBOR required submitters to estimate a rate at which the bank 'could' borrow — not a single objectively true rate — and because the government presented no evidence that Deutsche Bank could not have borrowed at the submitted rates, the submissions were not false under the wire fraud statute. The Second Circuit noted the conduct 'may have violated any reasonable notion of fairness' but did not satisfy the legal standard for fraud.
source1_description: United States v. Connolly, No. 19-3806 (2d Cir. Jan. 27, 2022) — Second Circuit opinion on Justia
source2_description: Reuters — U.S. Appeals Court Throws Out Deutsche Bank Traders' Libor-Rigging Convictions (January 27, 2022)
notes: This ruling was historic: the Second Circuit noted that with this reversal, every single U.S. trial conviction in LIBOR-rigging cases had been overturned. Connolly later sued Deutsche Bank for malicious prosecution, alleging the bank used him as a scapegoat and misled prosecutors; he subsequently settled that case in 2024. The reversal confirmed a sweeping limit on using wire fraud statutes to police financial benchmark-setting conduct.
disclaimer: CHARGED ONLY — NOT CONVICTED. Charges were filed but did not result in a conviction. (Trial conviction reversed; judgment of acquittal entered by Second Circuit.)

name: Gavin Campbell Black
charges_filed: Conspiracy to commit wire fraud and bank fraud (18 U.S.C. § 1349); wire fraud (18 U.S.C. § 1343) — same LIBOR manipulation scheme as co-defendant Connolly
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Southern District of New York — Federal; appeal: U.S. Court of Appeals, Second Circuit
case_number: United States v. Connolly, No. 19-3945 (2d Cir. 2022) / S.D.N.Y.
resolution_details: Convicted jointly with Matthew Connolly at trial in October 2018 (6 months home confinement, $100,000 penalty). The Second Circuit reversed the conviction on January 27, 2022, on identical grounds — insufficient evidence of falsity in LIBOR submissions — and ordered a judgment of acquittal entered. Black is a UK citizen based in London who was extradited for trial. He subsequently filed a malicious prosecution lawsuit against Deutsche Bank in New York state court for approximately $30 million.
source1_description: Levine Lee LLP Client Alert — Levine Lee LLP Secures Judgment of Acquittal for Gavin Black in LIBOR Case (January 27, 2022)
source2_description: Reuters — Exonerated Trader Settles $150 Million Lawsuit Against Deutsche Bank (July 10, 2024) [re: Connolly's parallel suit; Black's suit also noted]
notes: UK citizen tried in New York. The Second Circuit's Connolly/Black ruling closed out the U.S. government's LIBOR prosecution program; every individual trial-level conviction had been overturned. Both Connolly and Black have pursued civil claims against Deutsche Bank alleging it misled prosecutors and used them as scapegoats.
disclaimer: CHARGED ONLY — NOT CONVICTED. Charges were filed but did not result in a conviction. (Trial conviction reversed; judgment of acquittal entered by Second Circuit.)

name: Gordon J. Coburn
charges_filed: Conspiracy to violate the Foreign Corrupt Practices Act (FCPA) anti-bribery provisions; substantive FCPA anti-bribery violation; FCPA books-and-records violation — for allegedly authorizing a $2 million bribe to an Indian government official in Tamil Nadu to obtain a construction permit for a Cognizant campus in Chennai in 2014
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, District of New Jersey (Newark) — Federal
resolution_details: Charged in February 2019, Coburn pleaded not guilty and contested the charges for approximately six years. The case was repeatedly delayed by COVID-19, pretrial litigation, and a judicial recusal. On February 10, 2025, President Trump signed an executive order pausing FCPA criminal enforcement and directing DOJ to review pending FCPA cases. On April 1, 2025, U.S. Attorney Alina Habba moved to dismiss all charges under Federal Rule of Criminal Procedure 48(a), citing the executive order's application to the case and concluding that 'further prosecution is not in the interests of the United States.' U.S. District Judge Michael E. Farbiarz approved the dismissal with prejudice on April 3, 2025, making this the first criminal case formally dismissed under the FCPA enforcement pause.
source1_description: DOJ FCPA Case Page — United States v. Gordon J. Coburn, Court Docket Number: 19-CR-120-KM
source2_description: Baker Donelson Legal Alert — DOJ Dismisses FCPA Charges Against Former Cognizant Executives Following New Policy (April 9, 2025)
notes: The dismissal was explicitly policy-based — the government did not evaluate or concede the merits. Both the DOJ and SEC (which brought a parallel civil FCPA case) dismissed their actions. Cognizant separately settled with the SEC for $25 million and received a DOJ declination. Coburn's dismissal was with prejudice, barring any future federal prosecution on these charges.
disclaimer: CHARGED ONLY — NOT CONVICTED. Charges were filed but did not result in a conviction. (Charges were dismissed with prejudice by DOJ pursuant to executive order policy review, not on the merits.)

name: Steven E. Schwartz
charges_filed: Conspiracy to violate FCPA anti-bribery provisions; substantive FCPA anti-bribery violation; FCPA books-and-records violation — same bribery scheme as co-defendant Coburn (alleged authorization and concealment of $2 million bribe to Tamil Nadu official)
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, District of New Jersey (Newark) — Federal
resolution_details: Jointly charged and dismissed with Gordon Coburn on identical grounds. Schwartz, as Cognizant's general counsel, allegedly directed U.S.-based subordinates to conceal the bribe payment by falsifying change orders. Charged in February 2019, he pleaded not guilty. After nearly six years of litigation, all charges were dismissed with prejudice on April 3, 2025, pursuant to the Trump administration's FCPA enforcement pause executive order.
source1_description: DOJ FCPA Case Page — United States v. Gordon J. Coburn, Court Docket Number: 19-CR-120-KM (includes Schwartz)
source2_description: Reuters — US SEC Ends Cognizant Bribery Case That the Justice Department Also Abandoned (July 15, 2025)
notes: As Chief Legal Officer, Schwartz faced unique exposure for allegedly directing subordinates to conceal the bribe through falsified contractor change orders. The SEC's civil action against both Schwartz and Coburn was also subsequently dismissed on July 16, 2025, with the SEC citing policy rather than merits as justification — an unusual and explicit acknowledgment by the regulator.
disclaimer: CHARGED ONLY — NOT CONVICTED. Charges were filed but did not result in a conviction. (Charges were dismissed with prejudice by DOJ pursuant to executive order policy review, not on the merits.)
